title: Listening - Part 4: Mountain Rescue Volunteering
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Alice Wong, a mountain rescue volunteer. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today we’re joined by Alice Wong, a volunteer on a mountain rescue team. Alice, what made you become involved? || Alice: I enjoyed hillwalking for years, but I assumed rescue teams were for expert climbers. Then, during a walk, a member of my group injured an ankle. [24]A rescue team calmly helped us off the hill, and seeing their patience made me want to give something back[/24]. I discovered they needed people with many skills, not only climbing specialists, and I applied for training. || Presenter: What did the training teach you? || Alice: We learned navigation, first aid and how to use equipment safely. However, the biggest adjustment was not technical. [25]I had been used to making decisions for myself outdoors, whereas rescue work requires you to communicate constantly and act as part of a team[/25]. No one should wander off with a brilliant personal idea when safety depends on coordination. || Presenter: Are most rescues dramatic? || Alice: Television tends to show helicopters in storms, but the reality is usually quieter. [26]Many call-outs involve walkers who are exhausted, have become lost as darkness approaches, or simply need support to get down safely[/26]. These situations can still become serious, of course, which is why reaching people early matters. A straightforward rescue can also take hours when the ground is steep or slippery. || Presenter: Has technology made the hills safer? || Alice: Mobile phones and map apps are helpful, provided batteries last and there is a signal. Yet they can create false confidence. [27]A phone is useful in an emergency, but it cannot replace checking the forecast, taking suitable clothing and knowing your route[/27]. We sometimes meet people who have brought no warm layer because an app seemed reassuring. || Presenter: What is emotionally hardest about the job? || Alice: People expect us to act immediately, and we want to. But conditions can put rescuers in danger too. [28]It is difficult telling worried relatives that we may have to wait until wind or visibility improves before moving into a dangerous area[/28]. Rushing in would risk creating additional casualties. || Presenter: What gives you most satisfaction? || Alice: A successful rescue is obviously rewarding, but prevention is even better. [29]After our talks at schools and walking clubs, people often tell us they now carry equipment or plan routes more carefully[/29]. Knowing they may avoid needing a rescue is a real achievement. || Presenter: What would you say to somebody interested in joining? || Alice: Don’t join expecting constant excitement. [30]The team needs people prepared to train regularly, clean equipment and answer calls on wet, inconvenient evenings[/30]. If you like reliable service and teamwork, it is enormously worthwhile.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Alice decide to join mountain rescue?

options.A: She wanted to improve her climbing skills.
options.B: She was impressed when a team helped her group.
options.C: A team needed somebody with medical training.

questions.explanation: After rescuers calmly helped an injured member of her group, Alice wanted to contribute.

questions.question: What was the biggest change for Alice during training?

options.A: Learning to work through decisions with others.

questions.explanation: She had previously made her own outdoor decisions but had to learn constant team coordination.

questions.question: What does Alice say about many rescue operations?

options.A: They involve relatively ordinary difficulties for walkers.
options.B: They usually require helicopters in severe weather.
options.C: They happen because people refuse advice from teams.

questions.explanation: Many incidents involve tired or lost walkers needing help down safely.

questions.question: What is Alice's view of mobile phones on the hills?

options.A: They have made planning a walk unnecessary.
options.B: They are useful but are not a substitute for preparation.
options.C: They usually create more emergencies than they prevent.

questions.explanation: She says a phone can help in an emergency, but preparation and appropriate clothing remain essential.

questions.question: What situation does Alice find especially difficult?

options.A: Rescuing people who have ignored warnings.
options.B: Explaining why a rescue may need to be delayed.
options.C: Asking other team members to take risks.

questions.explanation: Poor conditions may mean she has to tell anxious relatives that the team must wait.

questions.question: Why does Alice value giving safety talks?

options.A: They encourage new volunteers to join.
options.B: They provide extra funding for equipment.
options.C: They may prevent emergencies from occurring.

questions.explanation: People tell the team that talks made them prepare better, potentially avoiding rescue.

questions.question: What quality does Alice say volunteers need?

options.A: A willingness to do routine work in inconvenient conditions.
options.B: Experience of leading climbs in difficult weather.
options.C: The ability to remain calm in front of cameras.

questions.explanation: She stresses regular training, cleaning equipment and responding in bad weather.
